frameworks.name: policy complexity
frameworks.one_line: policies indexed by mutual information between states and actions constrain memory and decoding time

frameworks.name: rate-distortion
frameworks.one_line: information-theoretic framing of bounded policies trading reward against information cost

frameworks.name: speed-accuracy trade-off
frameworks.one_line: trade-off reinterpreted as consequence of decoding cost of high-complexity policies

frameworks.name: set-size effects
frameworks.one_line: response-time growth with state-set size explained by complexity demands

core_question: Do joint time and memory costs on policy complexity jointly produce speed-accuracy trade-offs and set-size effects in human action selection?
key_assumptions: policies are state-to-action mappings whose memory equals mutual information between states and actions
higher-complexity policies preserve more state information and yield greater reward
high-complexity policies incur longer decoding time
manipulations: environmental regularities exploitable by low-complexity policies
key_findings: humans modulate policy complexity in response to time costs
humans modulate policy complexity in response to memory costs
response times grow with state-set size as predicted by complexity
trade-off between speed and accuracy tracks complexity manipulation
interpretations: speed-accuracy trade-offs may arise from decoding cost of complex policies
set-size effects may reflect complexity constraints rather than purely perceptual capacity limits
connecting_signals: mutual information between states and actions
